confirmed_findings.title: v1 auto-ships a 'default' row for every sis.available_sorters(); v2 only ships 6 curated sorters
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 extends contents with `[sorter, 'default', sis.get_default_sorter_params(sorter)] for sorter in sis.available_sorters()` so the Lookup table contains a 'default' row for every SI-registered sorter (~18 sorters: combinato, hdsort, herdingspikes, ironclust, kilosort, kilosort2, kilosort2_5, kilosort3, klusta, mountainsort4, mountainsort5, pykilosort, spykingcircus, spykingcircus2, tridesclous, tridesclous2, waveclus, waveclus_snippets, yass), populated with SI's canonical default kwargs dict.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 ships only 7 explicit rows: 2 MS4 presets (tetrode/cortex), 1 MS5 row, 1 KS4 row, 1 SC2 row, 1 TDC2 row, 1 clusterless_thresholder row. There is no fallback insertion loop. A v2 user wanting a kilosort2_5/ironclust/tridesclous default must insert it themselves; `_get_sorter_schema` will return `GenericSorterParamsSchema` (extra='allow') for them.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2's `SorterParameters` docstring at sorting.py:77-86 explicitly says: 'Users can insert additional rows for any installed SI sorter; the generic extra="allow" schema is the fallback dispatch for non-default sorters, preserving v1\'s "try any installed sorter" escape hatch.' Generic schema's docstring at _params/sorter.py:186-196 notes: 'Preserves v1\'s "try any installed SpikeInterface sorter" escape hatch without auto-inserting defaults for every sorter.' Generic schema correctly returns for non-curated names (test_params_validation.py:198-205).
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: v2 deliberately replaced silent ~18-row dynamic-insert with explicit per-sorter Pydantic dispatch + user-inserts-as-needed. The justification is shipping less; cost is breakage for v1 code that used a sorter via the auto-default name like ('kilosort2_5','default').

verdict.reasoning: All citations verified directly against the source. v1/sorting.py:184-189 contains the dynamic `contents.extend([[sorter, "default", sis.get_default_sorter_params(sorter)] for sorter in sis.available_sorters()])` loop, so v1 auto-ships a 'default' row for every SI-registered sorter. v2/sorting.py:106-183 ships exactly 7 explicit `_DEFAULT_CONTENTS` rows across 6 curated sorters (MS4 tetrode + MS4 cortex, MS5, KS4, SC2, TDC2, clusterless_thresholder) with no fallback insertion loop. The title's "6 curated sorters" matches unique sorter names; the body's "7 explicit rows" matches row count (MS4 has 2 presets). The v2 SorterParameters docstring (sorting.py:77-86) explicitly documents the design choice: 'Users can insert additional rows for any installed SI sorter; the generic extra="allow" schema is the fallback dispatch for non-default sorters, preserving v1\'s "try any installed sorter" escape hatch.' The GenericSorterParamsSchema docstring (_params/sorter.py:186-196) repeats the justification: 'Preserves v1\'s "try any installed SpikeInterface sorter" escape hatch without auto-inserting defaults for every sorter.' _get_sorter_schema at _params/sorter.py:209-230 correctly returns GenericSorterParamsSchema for non-curated names, and test_params_validation.py:198-205 explicitly tests this fallback for hdsort/herding_spikes. The classification (intentional_justified) is correct: this is a deliberate, documented design shift from silent auto-insert to explicit-rows + user-supplied fallback. Severity medium is appropriate — v1 code that referenced an auto-default like ('kilosort2_5','default') will fail at sort time on v2 unless the user inserts that row themselves; this is a real but well-documented migration cost.

confirmed_findings.title: Franklab MS4 preset row names renamed (uppercase 'KHz' to lowercase 'kHz' + '_ms4' suffix); breaks v1 lookups by name
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 ships `'franklab_tetrode_hippocampus_30KHz'` and `'franklab_probe_ctx_30KHz'` (capital K) and these are the canonical Frank-lab MS4 preset names referenced from notebooks, scripts and pipelines.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 ships `'franklab_tetrode_hippocampus_30kHz_ms4'` and `'franklab_probe_ctx_30kHz_ms4'` (lowercase k, plus a `_ms4` suffix to disambiguate from MS5).
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 comment at sorting.py:117-123 documents the cortex rename: 'Name normalized to v2\'s lowercase-k convention.' But the docstring does not justify the choice itself (only describes it), and any v1 code that does `(SpikeSorterParameters & {"sorter_params_name": "franklab_tetrode_hippocampus_30KHz"})` against the v2 table returns empty. v2 also adds `_ms4` / `_ms5` suffix so a v1 caller wouldn't find the row even with case normalized.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: The `_ms4`/`_ms5` suffix disambiguates between sorter versions for the same preset; the lowercase-k normalization is stylistic. Both are intentional but rename without a back-compat alias means hand-maintained v1 references silently miss.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly. v1/sorting.py:158 and :163 ship `franklab_tetrode_hippocampus_30KHz` and `franklab_probe_ctx_30KHz` (capital K, no sorter suffix). v2/sorting.py:109 and :125 ship `franklab_tetrode_hippocampus_30kHz_ms4` and `franklab_probe_ctx_30kHz_ms4` (lowercase k, with `_ms4` suffix). A grep of the codebase confirms no alias/back-compat row exists for the v1 capital-K names in v2; any v1 caller doing a name lookup against v2 (e.g., `(SorterParameters & {"sorter_params_name": "franklab_tetrode_hippocampus_30KHz"})`) gets an empty restriction. The `_ms4` suffix is justified by the coexistence of an MS5 row at v2:135 (`..._30kHz_ms5`). The lowercase-k rename is documented at v2:117-123 only as "Name normalized to v2's lowercase-k convention" — describing, not justifying, the choice or the back-compat break. Internal callers were updated (pipeline.py:69, 75; tests use the new names) so the v2 surface itself is consistent, but external/notebook code referencing v1 names by string will silently miss. Severity medium: silent empty-restriction on a string lookup is a foot-gun, not a correctness corruption, and is bounded to hand-maintained external references.

confirmed_findings.title: MS4 schema `adjacency_radius` constraint `ge=0.0` rejects SI's documented sentinel value of -1 ('use all channels')
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 stores `adjacency_radius: 100` (int) in `mountain_default`. SI's `get_default_sorter_params('mountainsort4')` returns `adjacency_radius: -1` (sentinel: use all channels in graph). v1 stored either value verbatim without validation; a user could insert any int.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 schema declares `adjacency_radius: float = Field(default=100.0, ge=0.0)`. A user trying to insert SI's `-1` sentinel ('use all channels') hits `ValidationError: Input should be greater than or equal to 0`. Verified by direct call: `MountainSort4Schema(adjacency_radius=-1)` raises `greater_than_equal` error.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Direct pydantic test: `MountainSort4Schema(adjacency_radius=-1)` raises. There is no comment in `_params/sorter.py` justifying the `ge=0.0` choice, and no test exercises the SI '-1 means all channels' use case. The schema docstring at lines 35-52 explains `extra='forbid'` for typo-catching but says nothing about ranging out SI's sentinel.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Validation tightening likely intended to catch nonsensical negative radii, but in doing so the schema implicitly bans SI's documented 'use all channels' encoding. A v1 user with `adjacency_radius=-1` in their params dict cannot port to v2.

verdict.reasoning:
All four claim components verified directly:

1. v1 file:line confirmed (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/sorting.py:147): `mountain_default` stores `"adjacency_radius": 100` verbatim in a `blob` column with no Pydantic validation — any int (including -1) could be inserted.

2. v2 file:line confirmed (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_params/sorter.py:57): `adjacency_radius: float = Field(default=100.0, ge=0.0)`.

3. Pydantic rejection reproduced. Constructing `MountainSort4Schema(adjacency_radius=-1)` with the exact schema definition from the file raises `ValidationError: Input should be greater than or equal to 0 [type=greater_than_equal, input_value=-1, input_type=int]`. This matches the claim's prediction exactly.

4. No documented justification in v2. The `MountainSort4Schema` docstring (lines 36-52) explains `extra='forbid'` for typo-catching but says nothing about banning SI's `-1` "use all channels" sentinel. Grep across `src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/` shows no comment mentioning `-1`, "use all channels", or "sentinel" for `adjacency_radius`.

5. No test coverage for the -1 sentinel. Grep across `tests/spikesorting/v2/` returns only three hits, all using the value `100.0` (`_smoke_constants.py:66`, `test_params_validation.py:212`, `test_parity_canonical.py:207`). No test inserts -1 or any negative value, and no test exercises the "use all channels" SI encoding.

Classification `intentional_unjustified` is correct: `ge=0.0` is an intentional schema choice (not drift — v1 had no validation), but the schema docs/comments do not justify why SI's documented sentinel is forbidden. Severity `medium` is reasonable — it breaks a v1-to-v2 portability path for a documented SI feature, but it is a Lookup-table insertion concern (caught at insert time, not silently corrupting outputs) and Frank-lab production presets use 100, not -1.

Minor caveat: the claim asserts SI documents -1 as "use all channels" for MS4. I did not independently verify the current SI 0.104 wrapper still honors -1 (only verified that the v2 schema rejects it). This does not change the verdict — the rejection of a value v1 accepted is real regardless of SI's interpretation of -1.

confirmed_findings.title: MS4 schema's `freq_min`/`freq_max` defaults silently choose v1's tetrode preset values (600/6000) over SI's MS4 defaults (300/6000)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1's `mountain_default` block does NOT include `freq_min` / `freq_max`; those keys come from per-row presets: `freq_min=600/freq_max=6000` for the tetrode preset and `freq_min=300/freq_max=6000` for the cortex preset. v1's auto-shipped `mountainsort4`/`default` row uses SI defaults `freq_min=300, freq_max=6000`.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 schema bakes `freq_min: float = Field(default=600.0)` and `freq_max: float = Field(default=6000.0)` directly into `MountainSort4Schema`. So a user calling `MountainSort4Schema()` with no args gets the *tetrode* preset values, not SI's MS4 wrapper defaults. The cortex preset row still overrides `freq_min=300` explicitly, so the cortex preset itself is correct.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Schema docstring at _params/sorter.py:38-51 explicitly explains: 'the mountain_default block itself did NOT include freq_min/freq_max -- those keys came from the tetrode preset; v2\'s schema-level defaults choose the tetrode preset values so a user constructing MountainSort4Schema() without arguments gets v1\'s most-used Frank-lab production preset implicitly.' Test at test_params_validation.py:208-218 pins the defaults to match `mountain_default` but does not pin the freq range to tetrode-preset values explicitly.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Author chose tetrode-preset values (Frank-lab production default) over SI's wrapper defaults. Justified for the lab; documented in the schema docstring.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all cited file:line references directly.

v1 confirmation (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/sorting.py):
- Lines 145-153: `mountain_default` dict contains detect_sign, adjacency_radius, filter, whiten, num_workers, clip_size, detect_threshold, detect_interval — NO `freq_min`/`freq_max` keys.
- Line 159: tetrode preset row `{**mountain_default, "freq_min": 600, "freq_max": 6000}`.
- Line 164: cortex preset row `{**mountain_default, "freq_min": 300, "freq_max": 6000}`.

v2 confirmation (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_params/sorter.py):
- Line 58: `freq_min: float = Field(default=600.0, gt=0.0, le=15000.0)`.
- Line 59: `freq_max: float = Field(default=6000.0, gt=0.0, le=15000.0)`.
- These are schema-level defaults, so `MountainSort4Schema()` returns the tetrode preset frequencies, not SI's wrapper defaults.

Rationale documented at lines 38-51 of the schema file: explicitly states that v1's `mountain_default` block did NOT include freq_min/freq_max, that those came from the tetrode preset, and that v2 chose tetrode-preset values so `MountainSort4Schema()` with no args yields Frank-lab production preset.

Test coverage check (tests/spikesorting/v2/test_params_validation.py:208-218): asserts MS4 defaults for detect_sign, adjacency_radius, filter, whiten, num_workers, clip_size, detect_threshold, detect_interval. Does NOT assert `freq_min`/`freq_max` values — matching the claim that the tetrode-preset choice is not explicitly pinned by this test. Other tests in the test suite reference 300.0/6000.0 (cortex preset) for preprocessing freq_min/freq_max, not the MS4 sorter schema defaults.

Cortex preset (v1 line 164 → v2 line should still override): the claim states cortex preset still overrides explicitly. Confirmed cortex preset in v1 sets freq_min=300 explicitly; v2 schema's baseline tetrode default would be overridden on cortex preset insert (not directly inspected here but consistent with the per-row contents).

Classification: intentional_justified. The divergence is real, documented in the docstring, and reflects a deliberate Frank-lab production-preset choice. The schema docstring also notes that constructing MountainSort4Schema() yields the tetrode preset implicitly — that's a deliberate UX decision, not drift. Severity remains low because (1) the choice is documented, (2) preset rows still override explicitly, and (3) it only affects users who construct MountainSort4Schema() with no overrides and don't read the docstring.

Note: I did not independently verify SI MS4 wrapper's `default_params` to confirm 300/6000 as SI defaults; the claim asserts this. The core divergence (v1 mountain_default has no freq keys; v2 schema bakes tetrode values) is independently verified.

confirmed_findings.title: MS4 schema `freq_min`/`freq_max` ranged with `le=15000.0` rejects high-sampling-rate use cases
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 stores `freq_min`/`freq_max` as integers with no range validation. A user wanting `freq_max=20000` for a 96-kHz recording could insert it.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 schema constrains `freq_min: float = Field(gt=0.0, le=15000.0)` and `freq_max: float = Field(gt=0.0, le=15000.0)`. A user trying to insert `freq_max=20000` for a high-sample-rate recording gets `ValidationError`. Verified: `MountainSort4Schema(freq_min=20000)` raises `less_than_equal` error.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Direct pydantic test confirms rejection. No docstring justifies the 15 kHz ceiling; it's a Nyquist-for-30-kHz constraint but the lab sampling rate is not part of the schema context.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Likely defense-in-depth for the lab's standard 30 kHz Intan/Trodes recordings (Nyquist=15 kHz). For unusual sampling rates this is a silent breakage.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified divergence directly. v1/sorting.py:159,164 stores freq_min/freq_max as integers inside a blob `sorter_params` dict with no range validation (DataJoint blob column, accepts any value). v2 _params/sorter.py:58-59 defines `freq_min: float = Field(default=600.0, gt=0.0, le=15000.0)` and `freq_max: float = Field(default=6000.0, gt=0.0, le=15000.0)`. A user passing freq_max=20000 (valid in v1) would get a pydantic `less_than_equal` ValidationError in v2.

Docstring at sorter.py:38-51 documents the defaults and `extra="forbid"` policy but does NOT justify the 15 kHz ceiling. There is no mention of Nyquist, sampling_frequency, or the 30 kHz Frank-lab standard in the schema file. The preset names (`franklab_tetrode_hippocampus_30KHz`, `franklab_probe_ctx_30KHz`) imply a 30 kHz assumption, which makes 15 kHz the Nyquist limit, but this reasoning is implicit, not coded or documented.

Test coverage of the upper bound is absent. I grepped tests/spikesorting/v2/ for "15000", "20000", "le=15000", "less_than_equal" against MS4 freq fields. Existing tests cover: (a) `gt=0` lower bound (test_scaffold.py:38-41), (b) cross-field freq_min < freq_max (test_params_validation.py:49-52, test_scaffold.py:44-47), (c) MS4 default values match v1 (test_params_validation.py:208-218). None exercise the `le=15000.0` ceiling. So the branch where a user passes freq_max=20000 (intending a higher-sample-rate recording) is untested.

Classification: intentional but unjustified in code (the bound is explicit, not accidental, but no docstring/comment ties it to Nyquist for the lab's 30 kHz standard; v2's stated policy is documented justifications). Real behavioral divergence vs v1.

Severity: low. The MS4 freq_max default is 6000 Hz, far below 15000. Frank-lab production recordings are 30 kHz (Nyquist=15 kHz), so realistic MS4 users will not hit the ceiling. Impact is limited to hypothetical higher-sample-rate users, who would also need to adjust other pipeline assumptions. Low matches the original claim.

confirmed_findings.title: MS4 schema's `detect_threshold` typed as `float`/`gt=0.0`; v1 stored it as an int
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 `mountain_default` stores `detect_threshold: 3` (int). v1 has no validation; a user could insert `detect_threshold=0` or a negative value.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 schema `detect_threshold: float = Field(default=3.0, gt=0.0)`. The default normalizes to `3.0` (float), and `0` is rejected. Verified: `MountainSort4Schema(detect_threshold=0)` raises `greater_than` error.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Direct pydantic check confirms rejection. SI MS4's downstream code likely treats `3` and `3.0` identically (Python ducks ints and floats), so the float coercion is benign. The `gt=0.0` rules out zero/negative thresholds; no v1 user case sets detect_threshold to 0 so impact is purely defense-in-depth.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Type tightening to documented float; gt=0.0 rules out nonsensical zero/negative thresholds. Not documented in the schema.

verdict.reasoning: Verified both citations directly. v1 sorting.py:152 stores `"detect_threshold": 3` (int literal) inside the raw `mountain_default` dict with no validation. v2 _params/sorter.py:64 declares `detect_threshold: float = Field(default=3.0, gt=0.0)`, normalizing to float and rejecting non-positive values. The pydantic `gt=0.0` constraint is a real behavioral divergence: v1 would accept `detect_threshold=0` or negative values without complaint, v2 raises a ValidationError. Test coverage exists for the default match (test_params_validation.py:208-218 `test_ms4_default_matches_v1_mountain_default` asserts 3.0) and for type validation (test_integrity.py:282-286 rejects "not_a_float"), though there is no explicit test that `detect_threshold=0` raises — the pydantic constraint is a structural guarantee. The schema docstring (sorter.py:50-52) mentions only `extra="forbid"` as the v1-vs-v2 rationale; the `gt=0.0` tightening is not explicitly justified in the docstring, matching the finding's claim that the rationale is undocumented. Float coercion of int defaults is a no-op for SpikeInterface's downstream numeric code (Python int/float duck-typing). Reclassifying as intentional_justified rather than uncertain: the v2 stated goal is "correctness-first port", and validating that a spike-detection threshold is positive aligns with that goal; no realistic v1 workflow sets detect_threshold ≤ 0, so migration risk is effectively zero. Severity remains low — defense-in-depth only.

confirmed_findings.title: MS5 v2 schema strips ~13 fields that v1's `sis.get_default_sorter_params('mountainsort5')` row carries
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1's MS5 default row is `sis.get_default_sorter_params('mountainsort5')`, which returns 20 fields: `detect_sign, detect_threshold, detect_time_radius_msec, filter, freq_max, freq_min, npca_per_channel, npca_per_subdivision, scheme, scheme1_detect_channel_radius, scheme2_detect_channel_radius, scheme2_max_num_snippets_per_training_batch, scheme2_phase1_detect_channel_radius, scheme2_training_duration_sec, scheme2_training_recording_sampling_mode, scheme3_block_duration_sec, snippet_T1, snippet_T2, snippet_mask_radius, whiten`. A v1 user can edit any of these via the params blob.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2's MS5 schema has `extra='forbid'` and only 8 fields: `schema_version, scheme, detect_sign, detect_threshold, detect_time_radius_msec, snippet_T1, snippet_T2, scheme2_phase1_detect_channel_radius, scheme2_detect_channel_radius`. So 12 SI MS5 fields (filter, freq_min, freq_max, npca_per_channel, npca_per_subdivision, scheme1_detect_channel_radius, scheme2_max_num_snippets_per_training_batch, scheme2_training_duration_sec, scheme2_training_recording_sampling_mode, scheme3_block_duration_sec, snippet_mask_radius, whiten) are NOT customizable via the schema -- a user trying to insert them hits `extra_forbidden`. The final SI call falls back to SI's runtime defaults for stripped fields.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Direct dump: `MountainSort5Schema().model_dump()` yields the 8-field dict; running `MountainSort5Schema(filter=True)` (or any of the 12 dropped fields) would raise `extra_forbidden`. Schema docstring at _params/sorter.py:71-77 justifies the field-set choice: 'Defaults follow appendix.md § MountainSort 5 install + params... MS5 has no tempdir parameter... and assumes the input recording has already been bandpass-filtered and whitened by the upstream recording stage.' This makes `filter`/`freq_min`/`freq_max`/`whiten` redundant. The other 8 fields are stripped because the v2 plan chose not to curate them.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: MS5 upstream pipeline does bandpass + whiten in the recording stage, so MS5's internal filter/whiten flags are redundant. The 8 stripped scheme/npca/snippet_mask fields fall through to SI's defaults at sort time. A power-user wanting to tune `snippet_mask_radius` from 250 must hack `extra='forbid'`.

verdict.reasoning:
All elements of the claim verified directly against code:

1. **v1 location** (`src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/sorting.py:184-188`): Confirmed v1 auto-inserts a "default" row per available sorter via `sis.get_default_sorter_params(sorter)`. For MS5 this yields 20 fields.

2. **SI MS5 default field list**: Empirically confirmed by running `sis.get_default_sorter_params('mountainsort5')` — returns exactly 20 fields matching the claim's list verbatim: `detect_sign, detect_threshold, detect_time_radius_msec, filter, freq_max, freq_min, npca_per_channel, npca_per_subdivision, scheme, scheme1_detect_channel_radius, scheme2_detect_channel_radius, scheme2_max_num_snippets_per_training_batch, scheme2_phase1_detect_channel_radius, scheme2_training_duration_sec, scheme2_training_recording_sampling_mode, scheme3_block_duration_sec, snippet_T1, snippet_T2, snippet_mask_radius, whiten`.

3. **v2 location** (`src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_params/sorter.py:68-88`): Confirmed `MountainSort5Schema` declares 9 fields total (`schema_version, scheme, detect_sign, detect_threshold, detect_time_radius_msec, snippet_T1, snippet_T2, scheme2_phase1_detect_channel_radius, scheme2_detect_channel_radius`) with `model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")` at line 79. The claim says "8 fields" but lists 9 including `schema_version` — minor cosmetic count, the substance is right (8 SI-facing fields + 1 bookkeeping `schema_version`).

4. **`extra='forbid'` rejection**: Empirically confirmed — `MountainSort5Schema(filter=True)`, `MountainSort5Schema(snippet_mask_radius=250)`, and `MountainSort5Schema(whiten=False)` all raise `ValidationError`. So users cannot insert rows that override these 12 SI fields without bypassing the schema.

5. **Fall-through to SI defaults**: Confirmed at v2 sorting.py:565 — `sorter_params = dict(sorter_row["params"])` only carries the 9 validated schema fields; stripped fields are absent from the kwargs dispatched to `sis.run_sorter`, so SI uses its own defaults at sort time. The shipped v2 default row at sorting.py:136 uses `_validate_params(_get_sorter_schema("mountainsort5"), {})` (empty override dict), so it ships the schema defaults exactly.

6. **Docstring rationale**: Confirmed at _params/sorter.py:71-77 — explicitly states MS5 has no `tempdir` and "assumes the input recording has already been bandpass-filtered and whitened by the upstream recording stage". This justifies removing `filter`/`freq_min`/`freq_max`/`whiten` (4 of 12) on redundancy grounds. The other 8 stripped fields (npca_per_channel, npca_per_subdivision, scheme1_detect_channel_radius, scheme2_max_num_snippets_per_training_batch, scheme2_training_duration_sec, scheme2_training_recording_sampling_mode, scheme3_block_duration_sec, snippet_mask_radius) have no per-field rationale in the docstring — they are simply "not curated".

7. **Test coverage**: Grep across tests/spikesorting/ shows zero references to any of the 12 stripped field names. `test_params_validation.py:227` only asserts the 9 schema-default fields match the appendix. The typo-rejection test (`test_dedicated_sorter_schemas_reject_typos`, line 328-348) confirms `extra='forbid'` behavior, but does not test that a power-user attempt to set `snippet_mask_radius=300` raises — that case is uncovered.

**Classification**: `intentional_justified` is correct — the schema is the result of a deliberate plan-driven choice (the module docstring at lines 7-26 explicitly explains the strictness policy and references appendix.md). The `extra='forbid'` is a known typo-catching tradeoff, accepted at the cost of customization surface.

**Severity**: `medium` is appropriate. A user who wants to tune `snippet_mask_radius` (250 default, sometimes relevant for tetrode geometry) or `scheme2_training_duration_sec` (300s default, relevant for short recordings) hits `extra_forbidden` and must modify the schema. The fall-through behavior means defaults are sensible, so existing workflows produce correct results — but the lost flexibility relative to v1 is real.

confirmed_findings.title: KS4 schema selects only 5 KS4 fields (Th_universal/Th_learned/nblocks/max_cluster_subset/do_CAR) as typed defaults; rest pass through via `extra='allow'`
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1's KS4 default row would be `sis.get_default_sorter_params('kilosort4')`, which (in environments where KS4 is installed) returns ~30+ fields covering batch_size, nearest_chans, n_pcs, dminx, x_centers, etc. Every field is part of the stored params dict.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 schema only types 5 KS4 fields as named defaults and uses `extra='allow'` for the rest. The default row stores `{schema_version: 1, Th_universal: 9.0, Th_learned: 8.0, nblocks: 1, max_cluster_subset: 25000, do_CAR: True}` (6 keys). All other KS4 fields fall back to SI's per-version defaults at sort time, which could shift across SI upgrades without a Spyglass schema bump.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Schema docstring at _params/sorter.py:91-104 explicitly notes: 'this schema types the most-used knobs but uses extra="allow" so users who need an undocumented KS4 kwarg (batch_size, nearest_chans, etc.) can pass it through to SI without an upstream schema PR. confirmed_findings.title: v2 fixes v1 trailing-edge artifact mask off-by-one (last valid sample zeroed, last frame n-1 left unmasked)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: Back-gap trigger range is np.arange(artifact_removed_intervals_ind[-1][1], len(timestamps) - 1). Because _consolidate_intervals returns stop_index INCLUSIVE (uses searchsorted(side='right') - 1), starting the trigger at stop_ind[-1] (rather than stop_ind[-1] + 1) ZEROS the last valid sample of the trailing valid interval. Ending at len(timestamps) - 1 LEAVES the very last recording sample (frame n-1) UNMASKED even when it falls in an artifact region. The two bugs partially cancel for the very-end case but mis-account boundary samples in general.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: _apply_artifact_mask walks valid_times left-to-right with cursor = max(cursor, ve); trailing branch builds artifact range [searchsorted(timestamps, cursor), len(timestamps)). Includes frame n-1; first frame after last valid window is the first artifact frame. Off-by-one explicitly pinned by test_apply_artifact_mask_zeroes_artifact_frames at test_single_session_pipeline.py:2920-3020 (asserts last_artifact frame is zero and first valid frame is unchanged).
confirmed_findings.evidence: v1 line 386: 'len(timestamps) - 1' is the exclusive end of np.arange, so frame n-1 is never included in list_triggers. v1 line 385 starts at artifact_removed_intervals_ind[-1][1] (stop_ind[-1]); _consolidate_intervals at v1/recording.py:743 returns stop_index = searchsorted(side='right') - 1 (inclusive of the last sample of the valid window). v2 test at test_single_session_pipeline.py:2920-2925 explicitly documents 'pins the off-by-one boundary: the LAST artifact frame must be zeroed (the original code dropped it...)' and asserts both halves at 3001-3020.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: v2 docstring at sorting.py:996-1055 documents the rewrite for boundary correctness and SI 0.104 ms_before/ms_after semantics. Test enforces both: last_artifact frame zero AND first-valid frame unchanged.

verdict.reasoning: Verified all citations directly. v1/sorting.py:382-388 confirms the trailing trigger range is np.arange(artifact_removed_intervals_ind[-1][1], len(timestamps) - 1) — start is the INCLUSIVE stop index of the last valid interval (per _consolidate_intervals at v1/recording.py:741-744 which returns searchsorted(side='right') - 1), and the end is exclusive at len(timestamps)-1, so frame n-1 is never zeroed. The inner gap loop at v1:378 correctly uses +1 for the start, making the trailing branch internally inconsistent. v2/sorting.py:1014-1027 walks valid_times with cursor = max(cursor, ve), and the trailing branch sets end = len(timestamps) (line 1025) — frame n-1 is included; first artifact frame is searchsorted(timestamps, cursor), not cursor_index - 1. The pinning test at tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:2905-3020 explicitly asserts (a) last_artifact frame (artifact_end_f - 1) is zero (lines 3014-3020) and (b) the first valid frame artifact_end_f is unchanged (lines 3001-3009); the docstring at 2920-2924 names the off-by-one as the regression being pinned. The v2 docstring at sorting.py:995-1054 documents the rewrite and also flags an SI 0.104 boundary bug for ms_before/ms_after=0.0, justifying the None,None choice. Classification intentional_justified is correct. Severity downgraded from 'high' to 'medium' because the v1 defect only mis-handles boundary samples (the very last valid sample is zeroed, and frame n-1 escapes masking if it falls in an artifact region) — a real, pinned, documented correctness fix but with limited practical impact on sorter output relative to the schema-default class of bugs.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 _apply_artifact_mask does NOT sort valid_times before walking; v1 _consolidate_intervals does
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: _consolidate_intervals at v1/recording.py:734-735 checks if intervals are monotone and calls np.sort(intervals, axis=0) when not. Unsorted valid_times still produces a correct mask.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: _apply_artifact_mask iterates valid_times in caller order, using cursor = max(cursor, ve). Unsorted valid_times can leave gaps unmasked because the cursor advances past intermediate valid windows or produces wrong start frames when vs <= cursor. No sort, no monotonicity assertion.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2/sorting.py:1016 uses 'for vs, ve in valid_times' with no prior sort; cursor logic at 1022 ('cursor = max(cursor, ve)') only guarantees monotone-forward cursor, not correct masking. IntervalList.valid_times is conventionally sorted by ArtifactDetection writer, but downstream tooling that rewrites the row (or test fixtures constructing intervals directly) is not guarded against. No test asserts unsorted valid_times behavior.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Probably an assumption that upstream IntervalList is always sorted. If that assumption holds it is harmless, but v2 silently produces wrong masks where v1 silently produced correct masks. Worth either documenting the assumption with an assert, or sorting defensively.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified directly against the cited code.

v1 evidence (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:733-735): `_consolidate_intervals` explicitly checks `if not np.all(intervals[:-1] <= intervals[1:]): intervals = np.sort(intervals, axis=0)`. v1 is defensively sorted.

v2 evidence (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:1014-1027): the loop `for vs, ve in valid_times: ... cursor = max(cursor, ve)` walks intervals in caller order with no sort and no monotonicity assertion. The docstring (lines 995-1002) makes no mention of an ordering precondition.

Concrete failure mode on unsorted input: with valid_times=[[t3,t4],[t1,t2]] (t1<t2<t3<t4), the first iteration sets cursor=t4. On the second iteration, vs=t1 is not > cursor=t4, so no gap is appended, and cursor stays t4. The artifact gap [t0,t1) and the gap [t2,t3) are silently NOT masked. v1's sort would catch this; v2 silently produces a wrong mask.

Upstream guarantee partially mitigates risk: `ArtifactDetection._detect_artifacts` (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/artifact.py:978-1004) builds valid_times by cursor-walking artifact spans in order, so the IntervalList row written by the v2 pipeline IS sorted by construction. The "none" preset also writes a single span. So in the canonical happy path, no divergence occurs.

Test coverage check (tests/spikesorting/v2/): the only direct test, `test_apply_artifact_mask_zeroes_artifact_frames` (test_single_session_pipeline.py:2905-3006), constructs valid_times in sorted order ([[t0, t[100]], [t[200], t[-1]]]). I grepped all v2 tests for valid_times — none exercise an unsorted valid_times array against _apply_artifact_mask. The unsorted branch is untested.

Classification: real behavioral drift from v1, low real-world blast radius (canonical upstream produces sorted intervals), but the silent-wrong-mask failure mode for fixtures or third-party IntervalList writers is real. medium is defensible; low is also defensible. I'm keeping medium per the submitter since v1 was explicitly defensive and v2 dropped that defense without documenting the precondition.

Recommendation (not asked for, but salient): add either `assert np.all(np.diff(valid_times[:,0]) >= 0)` or a `valid_times = valid_times[np.argsort(valid_times[:,0])]` line and document the precondition in the docstring.

confirmed_findings.title: v1 crashes on empty valid_times; v2 silently masks the entire recording
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: _consolidate_intervals at line 747 dereferences start_indices[0] / stop_indices[0]; on an empty intervals array this raises IndexError before the masking branch is even evaluated. v1 effectively requires at least one valid interval.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: _apply_artifact_mask with empty valid_times produces frame_ranges = [], skips the for-loop, and then the trailing branch (cursor=timestamps[0] < timestamps[-1]) appends the entire recording to artifact_frames. The sort runs on an all-zeros recording and almost certainly returns zero units.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2/sorting.py:1015 sets cursor = timestamps[0]; with empty valid_times the loop body never executes, and 1023 'if cursor < timestamps[-1]' is true, masking everything. Compared with v1 raising on empty input, v2 silently degrades to zero-unit sort. No test covers this branch.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Likely uncovered edge case. A defensive 'if len(valid_times) == 0: raise ValueError' would match v1's noisy-failure semantics and avoid masking an entire recording.

verdict.reasoning:
Claim verified by direct code reads.

v1 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/sorting.py:359-361 → src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:741-747): `_consolidate_intervals` calls `np.searchsorted` on the empty intervals array and then immediately accesses `start_indices[0], stop_indices[0]` at line 747 → IndexError on empty input. Loud failure.

v2 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:1014-1027): With `valid_times` of shape (0, 2): `cursor = timestamps[0]`; `for vs, ve in valid_times` iterates 0 times; trailing branch `if cursor < timestamps[-1]` is True → appends `(0, len(timestamps))` to `frame_ranges`; `artifact_frames = np.arange(0, len(timestamps))`; `sip.remove_artifacts` zeros the entire recording. Sort runs on all-zeros → near-zero units, no error.

Upstream can legitimately produce empty valid_times: `ArtifactDetection._detect_artifacts` returns `np.empty((0, 2))` at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/artifact.py:1004 when min_length_s filtering drops every interval. `make_insert` (artifact.py:782-826) writes the empty array to IntervalList unconditionally. `Sorting.make_fetch` (sorting.py:499-512) fetches it as `obs_intervals`; `make_compute` (sorting.py:558) only guards `obs_intervals is not None`, never checks `len(...) == 0`. So an all-channels-flagged synthetic input flows straight to `_apply_artifact_mask` and silently masks the whole recording.

Note that v2/recording.py:1424 DOES raise on empty intersection for the Recording stage — establishing a precedent for fail-loud on empty intervals — making the missing guard in Sorting._apply_artifact_mask an inconsistency within v2 itself, not just a v1 divergence.

Test coverage check:
- `test_apply_artifact_mask_zeroes_artifact_frames` (tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:2905) uses 2 non-empty intervals; does NOT exercise empty input.
- `test_detect_artifacts_amplitude_and_zscore_combined` (tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:4196) DOES produce `valid_times.shape == (0, 2)` and even comments on it (line 4250), but asserts shape only — does not pipe the empty result through `_apply_artifact_mask` or the rest of the sort.
- No grep hit for `_apply_artifact_mask(... empty ...)` or `valid_times=np.empty((0,...))` in the v2 test tree.

Finding is real, divergent from v1 in a particularly nasty way (silent zero-unit sort instead of loud crash), within v2 inconsistent with the Recording.make empty-check at recording.py:1424, and not exercised by tests. Severity medium is justified: it doesn't corrupt downstream tables silently with wrong-but-plausible numbers (those would be high), but it does turn a noisy input bug into a silent zero-output result that a user would likely misread as "no good units in this session."

confirmed_findings.title: ArtifactDetection FK is nullable in v2; artifact_id=None skips masking entirely (new graceful path)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1's SpikeSortingSelection requires an IntervalList FK ('-> IntervalList'). The artifact-removed intervals come from a populated IntervalList row, and v1 always evaluates the masking branch (lines 364-397) even when the only valid interval covers the full recording.

verdict.reasoning: All cited code matches. v1 SpikeSortingSelection at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/sorting.py:198-206 has '-> IntervalList' as a non-nullable FK; the mask path at 364-397 always evaluates. v2 SortingSelection at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:218-220 has '-> [nullable] ArtifactDetection'; make_compute at 558-562 guards 'if sel_row.get("artifact_id") is not None and obs_intervals is not None'; _write_units_nwb at 1480-1486 falls back to '[timestamps[0], timestamps[-1]]' when obs_intervals is None. The v2 docstring at 208-213 documents this as an intentional shared-contracts pattern ("materializer lands... nullable FK... DataJoint enforces referential integrity"). Test coverage claims verified: test_sorting_populates_with_mountainsort5 at test_single_session_pipeline.py:1047 uses 'artifact_params_name="none"' with a real artifact_id (line 1084), exercising the mask path with a full-window valid interval, NOT the artifact_id=None branch. test_sorting_selection_artifact_id_none_is_distinct_identity at line 5107 pins identity semantics for artifact_id=None on SortingSelection.insert_selection but stops short of calling Sorting.populate. No grep hit shows Sorting.populate run end-to-end against an artifact_id=None row. Classification new_in_v2 / low severity is defensible: the no-mask path matches v1's no-op-IntervalList semantics (v1's mask branch at 364-368 short-circuits when the intervals already span the full recording), so behavior is equivalent for the equivalent input. Minor concern worth noting (not a refutation): the absence of an end-to-end Sorting.populate test with artifact_id=None means the new path could regress silently — coverage gap matches the finding's own evidence.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 introduces SortingAnalyzer with hardcoded analyzer-extension parameters (max_spikes_per_unit=500, waveforms ms_before=1.0/ms_after=2.0); v1 had none
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 captures only the SortingExtractor; no SortingAnalyzer, no random_spikes / noise_levels / templates / waveforms computation. NWB write contains only spike_times, id, obs_intervals, curation_label.

These values are HARDCODED in source; not exposed via SorterParameters, job_kwargs, or dj.config.

A user wanting longer waveform windows (e.g. neuropixel templates) must monkey-patch _build_analyzer. The choice of method='uniform' for random_spikes is also fixed.
