Test coverage: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_integrity.py:239-287 exercises `SorterParameters.insert1` with a bogus `detect_threshold` and asserts `pytest.raises((ValueError, TypeError), match="detect_threshold")`, confirming the `_validate_params` + `_assert_schema_version_matches` chain is covered.

Classification new_in_v2 is correct: these columns and the validation chain do not exist in v1. Severity medium is reasonable — the columns enable schema evolution and per-row n_jobs overrides, and the pydantic validation gate at insert time is the structural protection against the noise_levels=[1.0] class of bug. Not critical because it's additive (not a behavior change in shared paths) and is exercised by an integrity test.

Rationale (forward schema evolution + per-row job_kwargs override + pydantic-at-insert) is consistent with the docstring at v2/sorting.py:77-86 explaining the per-sorter schema dispatch and with the "Job-Kwargs Resolution" invariant comment at lines 570-578.

confirmed_findings.title: Sorting master adds `analyzer_folder` and `n_units` columns; varchar(40) bumped to varchar(72) for object_id; time_of_sort type changes from int (Unix) to datetime
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: Sorting master columns: `analysis_file_name, object_id: varchar(40), time_of_sort: int`. No analyzer_folder. No n_units.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: Sorting master columns: `analysis_file_name, object_id: varchar(72), analyzer_folder: varchar(255), n_units: int, time_of_sort: datetime`. The analyzer_folder is the binary SortingAnalyzer folder on disk (sparse=True); n_units is the cached unit count for fast existence checks.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 module docstring at v2/sorting.py:411-419: 'make() applies post-motion preprocessing, runs the sorter ... builds a SortingAnalyzer(format=binary_folder, sparse=True)'. The SortingAnalyzer API is the v2 design pivot. n_units serves as the zero-unit guard in `get_sorting` (v2/sorting.py:749-765).
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: SortingAnalyzer is the v2 architectural pivot; the analyzer folder is a persistent on-disk artifact. n_units cached so `get_sorting` can short-circuit to an empty NumpySorting without opening the units NWB (which has no spike_times column for zero-unit sorts). varchar(72) accommodates longer NWB object IDs; datetime is more readable than Unix epoch.

verdict.reasoning: All claims verified directly. v1/sorting.py:233-240 shows the v1 master table with `object_id: varchar(40), time_of_sort: int` (Unix). v2/sorting.py:422-430 shows the v2 master with `object_id: varchar(72), analyzer_folder: varchar(255), n_units: int, time_of_sort: datetime`. The class docstring at v2/sorting.py:411-419 explicitly documents the SortingAnalyzer(format="binary_folder", sparse=True) architecture, justifying the analyzer_folder column. The n_units column is used as a zero-unit guard at v2/sorting.py:749-765 in get_sorting, with a comment explaining that NwbSortingExtractor cannot open an empty units NWB (no spike_times column), so n_units==0 short-circuits to an empty NumpySorting — confirming the cached unit count rationale. The varchar(72) for object_id and datetime for time_of_sort are minor schema improvements consistent with the architectural rewrite. These are all intentional, documented, justified changes tied to the v2 SortingAnalyzer design pivot.

confirmed_findings.title: Zero-unit sort persists a phantom analyzer_folder path that points to a never-created directory
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 has no analyzer concept. On zero units, _write_sorting_to_nwb writes an empty Units NWB and the Sorting row carries (analysis_file_name, object_id, time_of_sort) only -- every persisted reference is to a real artifact on disk.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: On zero units, _build_analyzer returns the would-be folder path WITHOUT calling create_sorting_analyzer (sorting.py:1396-1403). make_insert then commits the master row with analyzer_folder=str(folder) (sorting.py:674) and n_units=0 -- the path column points to a directory that was NEVER created. Callers must read n_units first; get_analyzer guards (sorting.py:841-848), delete() guards via folder.exists() (sorting.py:957), but external code or external folder-discovery scripts that scan analyzer_folder paths would dereference a missing directory.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 sorting.py:1393-1395 comment: 'Return the (not yet created) folder path for the row; Sorting.get_analyzer raises ZeroUnitAnalyzerError for a zero-unit sort rather than trying to load this never-built folder.' Integration test at test_single_session_pipeline.py:3509-3512 asserts the path is a non-empty string but never checks Path(...).exists(). No test asserts whether external readers can trust the column.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Intentional per the inline comment, but the rationale ('get_analyzer guards it') only covers Spyglass callers. Storing a path to a non-existent folder is a design smell -- NULL or '' would be safer. Suggest changing the schema/insert to store NULL for zero-unit rows; the existing exists() guards in delete()/get_analyzer would still work.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly. sorting.py:1396-1403 returns the would-be folder path without calling create_sorting_analyzer when sorting.get_num_units() == 0. make_insert at sorting.py:674 unconditionally commits analyzer_folder=str(analyzer_folder). The inline comment at 1387-1395 explicitly documents this as intentional ("Return the (not yet created) folder path for the row; Sorting.get_analyzer raises ZeroUnitAnalyzerError"). The test at test_single_session_pipeline.py:3509-3512 asserts only isinstance(str) and truthy, with a comment at lines 3506-3508 explicitly acknowledging the design choice ("the stored analyzer_folder column is still a real path string (guard lives in get_analyzer, not the schema)"). No test asserts Path(analyzer_folder).exists() for zero-unit rows. Spyglass-side guards exist: get_analyzer (841-848) raises ZeroUnitAnalyzerError after fetching n_units == 0 before touching the folder; delete (957) checks folder.exists(); _rebuild_analyzer_folder is gated by get_analyzer. Severity reduced to low — design is documented in both code and test, all in-tree code paths handle it, impact limited to hypothetical external folder-discovery scripts. Classification intentional_unjustified is borderline: the rationale IS explicit but only covers Spyglass callers, so the unjustified label holds for the externals-trust angle.

confirmed_findings.title: NonIntegerUnitIDError has zero test coverage even though v2 explicitly added the guard
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 passes sorting.get_unit_ids() through directly to nwbf.add_unit(id=unit_id, ...) at sorting.py:594-598 without any int-cast or validation. Non-integer IDs propagate to pynwb which silently converts or raises a pynwb-shaped error -- no clear failure mode.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 _populate_unit_part wraps int(unit_id) in try/except at sorting.py:1610-1618 and raises NonIntegerUnitIDError with a clear message and a remap suggestion. The check is real production code; the guard exists in defense of the rationale that 'kilosort4 may return numpy.int64' (audit-task context).
confirmed_findings.evidence: grep -rn 'NonIntegerUnit' tests/spikesorting/v2/ returns no matches. The exception class is defined at v2/exceptions.py:33-36 but no test instantiates it or exercises the raise path. int(np.int64(x)) succeeds, so the practical kilosort4 scenario does NOT trigger this guard -- the guard exists for a hypothetical sorter (string IDs, UUIDs, etc.) that nobody has demonstrated.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: The guard reads as defensive programming for a case nobody has hit. Either build a test that constructs a synthetic NumpySorting with object-dtype unit_ids (e.g., strings) and asserts the raise, or document why the guard is unreachable in practice and remove it.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly: v1/sorting.py:592-599 passes sorter unit_ids to nwbf.add_unit(id=...) with no validation; v2/sorting.py:1609-1618 wraps int(unit_id) in try/except and raises NonIntegerUnitIDError defined at v2/exceptions.py:33-36 with a remap-suggestion message. grep -rn 'NonIntegerUnit' across tests/ returns zero matches — no test exercises the raise path. The only int(unit_id) usages in tests (baseline_capture.py:279, test_single_session_pipeline.py:2687) are successful conversions, not error-path tests. The audit rationale (kilosort4 numpy.int64) is unconvincing because int(np.int64(x)) succeeds; the guard only fires for hypothetical sorters returning string/UUID/object-dtype unit_ids, which no test demonstrates. Classification is correct as untested_branch, severity low — defensive code with a clean error path but no instantiation/coverage. Recommendation stands: either add a test constructing a NumpySorting with object-dtype unit_ids and asserting the raise, or document why the guard is unreachable and remove it.

confirmed_findings.title: Mode A cleanup (analyzer_folder removed when _write_units_nwb fails) is implemented but untested
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 has no analyzer concept and no Mode A. _save_sorting_results errors propagate up; the only artifact is the units NWB itself which is unlinked by AnalysisNwbfile's own cleanup if make_insert fails.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: make_compute wraps _write_units_nwb in try/except (sorting.py:607-626) and rmtrees analyzer_folder on failure. This branch is critical because the analyzer folder is 5-50 GB; a leak per failed populate is catastrophic. The Mode B equivalent (failure during make_insert) IS tested by test_sorting_make_rollback_cleans_units_nwb at line 3537+, but Mode A is not.
confirmed_findings.evidence: No test in tests/spikesorting/v2/ monkeypatches Sorting._write_units_nwb to raise. test_sorting_make_rollback_cleans_units_nwb (test_single_session_pipeline.py:3537) covers Mode B by monkeypatching _populate_unit_part. The Mode A try/except (sorting.py:614-626) including the nested cleanup-exception handler at 621-625 has no test, even though it is the disk-leak-critical path.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Same exact disk-leak risk as Mode B which IS tested; the asymmetry suggests Mode A was added later (audit-trail consistent with the file's structure) without backfilling its test.

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED. Verified by reading code and tests directly.

Evidence:
1. v2 sorting.py:607-626 contains the Mode A try/except: `_build_analyzer` runs first (line 599-605), then `_write_units_nwb` is wrapped in try/except (607-614); on Exception the analyzer_folder is rmtree'd (618-620) with a nested defensive logger (621-625). The comment block at 615-617 explicitly names this "Mode A cleanup" and says it prevents "a half-built scratch dir" from leaking.

2. v1 sorting.py:461-496 (`_save_sorting_results`) has no analyzer concept and no equivalent cleanup. v2 Mode A is therefore a v2-only safety branch — there is no v1 parity behavior to compare against.

3. The cited Mode B test, test_sorting_make_rollback_cleans_units_nwb (test_single_session_pipeline.py:3534-3679), confirms Mode B coverage: at line 3636-3643 it monkeypatches `Sorting._populate_unit_part` (not `_write_units_nwb`) to raise. Its assertion at 3674-3679 verifies the analyzer folder is removed by `make_insert`'s rollback path — i.e., Mode B specifically.

4. Grep across tests/spikesorting/v2/ for any patch of `_write_units_nwb` returns zero hits. `monkeypatch.setattr(Sorting, ...)` is used exactly once (line 3801) and targets `_run_sorter`, not `_write_units_nwb`. The only direct `_write_units_nwb` test (test_write_units_nwb_handles_zero_unit_sorter at 5459) calls it on the happy path with an empty sorting to test the zero-unit guard at sorting.py:822-826; it does not force the function to raise.

5. Asymmetry of risk: Mode A and Mode B both leak the same 5-50 GB analyzer folder (test_sorting_delete_removes_analyzer_folder at 6058-6099 documents the size). Mode B is the only one tested. The nested cleanup-exception handler at 621-625 (marked `# pragma: no cover -- defensive`) explicitly acknowledges the branch is uncovered even by the author.

Classification: untested_branch — code exists and appears correct, but no test exercises the failure path. Severity high because the leak is large per occurrence (5-50 GB) and silent (logged at error level, populate continues for other keys). Not critical because it does not affect scientific correctness and the trigger (`_write_units_nwb` raising) is uncommon in practice.

Suggested fix: add a test that monkeypatches `Sorting._write_units_nwb` to raise, then asserts `analyzer_folder` does not exist after populate (mirror of the Mode B test at 3537+).

confirmed_findings.title: _rebuild_analyzer_folder has no test coverage including the artifact_id sub-branch
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 has no concept of a rebuildable analyzer folder; on missing artifact files the user re-populates the table.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: get_analyzer (sorting.py:821-853) calls _rebuild_analyzer_folder when the folder is missing. _rebuild_analyzer_folder (sorting.py:855-922) re-reads the canonical sorting from the units NWB, re-applies the artifact mask if artifact_id is set, and rebuilds the analyzer. The rebuild path has its own DB I/O (sel_row fetch1, resolve_source, IntervalList fetch1 in the artifact branch) and its own cleanup exception handler.
confirmed_findings.evidence: grep -rn '_rebuild_analyzer_folder\|rebuild_analyzer' tests/spikesorting/v2/ returns no matches. Both branches (artifact_id is None and artifact_id is not None) are unreachable in tests. The cleanup except block at sorting.py:913-922 is also untested.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: The rebuild path is invoked silently inside get_analyzer; users in production who run a re-populate after manually deleting the scratch folder will hit it. Untested means the behavior is unverified -- including whether get_sorting(key) on a zero-unit sort would actually take this path (it would: _rebuild_analyzer_folder calls get_sorting which returns empty NumpySorting, then _build_analyzer short-circuits, then load_sorting_analyzer at sorting.py:853 would fail on the still-missing folder). The interaction with zero-unit sorts is the most concerning untested case.

verdict.reasoning: Verified the citations directly. sorting.py:821-853 shows get_analyzer raises ZeroUnitAnalyzerError early for n_units==0 (so the zero-unit-takes-rebuild concern in the rationale is partly off), then checks `folder.exists()` and calls `_rebuild_analyzer_folder` if missing. sorting.py:855-922 implements the rebuild: fetch sel_row, resolve_source (raises NotImplementedError for concat at line 871-874), Recording().get_recording(), then if sel_row['artifact_id'] is not None it does extra DB I/O (RecordingSelection.fetch1, IntervalList.fetch1) and applies the artifact mask, then calls get_sorting + _build_analyzer wrapped in a try/except that rmtrees the partial folder and re-raises. grep 'rebuild_analyzer\|_rebuild_analyzer_folder' across tests/spikesorting/v2/ returns zero matches. The only get_analyzer test (test_single_session_pipeline.py:1204-1219) takes the happy path with an existing folder. test_sorting_delete_removes_analyzer_folder (line 6058) deletes the row, not just the folder, so it doesn't trigger rebuild either. Neither artifact branch nor the concat NotImplementedError nor the cleanup except is exercised. Severity is correctly high: the rebuild path has its own DB I/O and an exception-handling invariant ("analyzer folder reflects canonical sort") that is unverified, and silent invocation inside get_analyzer means production users can hit it without warning. (Minor caveat: the rationale's specific zero-unit example is wrong because the n_units==0 check at line 841 short-circuits before the folder.exists() check at line 851; but the broader untested_branch claim stands.)

confirmed_findings.title: _apply_artifact_mask: empty valid_times silently masks the entire recording instead of failing
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 calls _consolidate_intervals(artifact_removed_intervals, timestamps) at sorting.py:359 which would crash on empty input at recording.py:747 (start_indices[0] on empty array raises IndexError). v1 fails loudly.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: _apply_artifact_mask at sorting.py:993-1055 iterates 'for vs, ve in valid_times'; an empty valid_times skips the loop. cursor=timestamps[0] then triggers the trailing 'if cursor < timestamps[-1]' branch (sorting.py:1023), generating a frame_range covering [0, n_samples). The entire recording is then masked to zeros and the sort runs on all-zero data -- silently producing a degenerate (zero-unit or near-zero-unit) sort.
confirmed_findings.evidence: No test in tests/spikesorting/v2/ passes valid_times of shape (0, 2) to _apply_artifact_mask. The 'all-frames-flagged-as-artifact' scenario IS produced by test_artifact_detection_or_combine (test_single_session_pipeline.py:4206 -- 'valid_times is empty (whole recording flagged as artifact) under OR'), but that test stops at the ArtifactDetection layer and never drives the empty valid_times into Sorting. The downstream consequence -- silently sorting all-zero data -- is unmeasured.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: v2's intent (per docstring) is to compute the complement of valid_times. Empty valid_times means 'no valid windows' which logically IS the whole recording as complement -- so the code's behavior is internally consistent. But the scientific intent of 'entire recording is artifact' is 'do not sort; emit zero units with a clear signal' -- not 'silently sort on zeros'. Recommend an early-return guard: if valid_times.size == 0, raise a clear error like NoValidArtifactWindowError. v2 has no such guard.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified by reading the cited code directly.

V2 behavior (sorting.py:993-1055) traced with `valid_times.shape == (0, 2)`:
- Line 1015: `cursor = timestamps[0]`
- Line 1016: `for vs, ve in valid_times:` -- empty iterable, loop body never executes
- Line 1023: `if cursor < timestamps[-1]:` -- TRUE
- Lines 1024-1027: `start = searchsorted(timestamps, timestamps[0]) = 0`, `end = len(timestamps)`, appends `(0, len(timestamps))` to `frame_ranges`
- Lines 1029-1055: builds `artifact_frames = np.arange(0, n_samples)` and passes to `sip.remove_artifacts` which zeros every sample.

V1 behavior (v1/recording.py:730-765, called from v1/sorting.py:359):
- Line 741: `start_indices = np.searchsorted(timestamps, intervals[:, 0], side="left")` -- if `intervals.shape == (0, 2)`, then `intervals[:, 0]` is shape (0,) and `start_indices` is shape (0,)
- Line 747: `start, stop = start_indices[0], stop_indices[0]` -- IndexError on empty array. V1 fails loudly. Confirmed.

No guard in v2 sorting.py for empty `valid_times`: `grep -n "valid_times.size\|valid_times.shape\|len(valid_times)\|len(obs_intervals)\|obs_intervals.size\|obs_intervals.shape"` returned no matches in src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py.

No docstring rationale documents the empty case as intentional — only an unrelated SI 0.104 ms_before/ms_after boundary comment.

Test coverage:
- `test_apply_artifact_mask_zeroes_artifact_frames` (test_single_session_pipeline.py:2905-2986) uses a two-row non-empty `valid_times`, not (0, 2).
- `test_detect_artifacts_amplitude_and_zscore_combined` (test_single_session_pipeline.py:4196-4257) DOES produce `valid_times.shape == (0, 2)` from `ArtifactDetection._detect_artifacts`, but only asserts the shape at the artifact-detection layer — it never feeds that empty array into `Sorting._apply_artifact_mask` or `Sorting.make_compute`.
- Downstream consequence amplifies the bug: v2 sorting.py:749-765 explicitly treats zero-unit sorts as "a valid result" with only `logger.warning`, so a sort on all-zero data could complete silently with a degenerate (zero or near-zero unit) result — no exception, no error log distinguishing it from a legitimate quiet recording.

The claim is accurate: v1 crashes loudly on empty input; v2 silently masks the entire recording to zeros and proceeds to sort, with the downstream zero-unit path swallowing the degenerate outcome without a clear error signal. Severity high is appropriate given the failure is silent and produces scientifically invalid results (sort run on synthesized zeros) that look indistinguishable from a quiet session.

File:line citations:
- src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:993-1055 (_apply_artifact_mask)
- src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:558-562 (make_compute call site, no guard)
- src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:749-765 (zero-unit silent acceptance amplifies impact)
- src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:741-747 (v1 IndexError on empty input)
- src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/sorting.py:359-361 (v1 call site to _consolidate_intervals)
- tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:4196-4257 (empty valid_times produced but not fed to Sorting)
- tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:2905-2986 (only non-empty valid_times tested in _apply_artifact_mask)

confirmed_findings.title: _run_si_sorter MS4 path mutates numpy globally (np.Inf = np.inf) with no teardown
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 does not mutate numpy. The spikeextractors compatibility issue did not exist at v1's pinned SI version (0.99) which preceded numpy 2.0's removal of np.Inf.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: Inside _run_si_sorter at sorting.py:1257-1258, the code does `_np.Inf = _np.inf` if sorter is mountainsort4 and `_np.Inf` is missing. This is a GLOBAL module mutation that persists for the lifetime of the process. There is no try/finally to remove the alias after the sort. Subsequent test runs or unrelated code see numpy with an undocumented Inf attribute they did not have before.
confirmed_findings.v1_location: n/a (v1 used SI 0.99, no MS4-on-numpy2 compat issue)
confirmed_findings.evidence: grep -rn 'np.Inf\|monkeypatch.*Inf' tests/spikesorting/v2/ returns no matches. The comment at sorting.py:1254-1256 says 'assignment is benign (np.inf is what np.Inf always meant) and survives subsequent calls' -- explicitly acknowledging the permanent mutation.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Intentional shim for a transitive dep (spikeextractors 0.9.11). The behavior is benign-but-permanent. The right fix is either upstream (pin spikeextractors to a fixed version) or use a try/finally with del _np.Inf for tighter scoping. Currently untested either way.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly. v2 sorting.py:1257-1258 inside `_run_si_sorter` executes `_np.Inf = _np.inf` when sorter is mountainsort4 and the attr is missing. The surrounding try/finally blocks (outer at 1247/1336 for tempdir, inner at 1322-1335 for global job kwargs) do not remove the alias; there is no `del _np.Inf` anywhere in the file. The comment at lines 1254-1256 explicitly states the mutation "survives subsequent calls", confirming the permanent process-wide effect is intentional. v1 sorting.py contains no `np.Inf` reference (confirmed via grep), so v1 does not mutate numpy — consistent with its pinned SI 0.99 / pre-numpy-2.0 era. Test coverage check: `grep -rn 'np.Inf|numpy.Inf|monkeypatch.*Inf'` over tests/spikesorting/v2/ returns no matches; the lone "Inf" hit is unrelated (peak RSS docs in a fixtures README). So the branch is untested in either direction — neither asserting persistence nor asserting cleanup. Classification intentional_unjustified is appropriate (deliberate shim with acknowledged permanence, no try/finally scoping, no test), severity low (the value np.inf is semantically identical to the removed np.Inf alias, so the leaked attribute is benign though architecturally unclean).

confirmed_findings.title: _run_si_sorter global job_kwargs restore-on-raise path is untested
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 does not use set_global_job_kwargs; it splatted sorter_params into sis.run_sorter directly at sorting.py:452-455.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: _run_si_sorter installs job_kwargs via si.set_global_job_kwargs (sorting.py:1305-1306) inside a try/finally; the finally at 1324-1335 restores the previous global. The success path is tested by test_run_si_sorter_restores_global_job_kwargs at test_single_session_pipeline.py:4001 (mocks run_sorter to return 'DUMMY'). The raise path -- run_sorter throws and the finally still must restore -- is untested.
confirmed_findings.evidence: test_run_si_sorter_restores_global_job_kwargs only exercises the success path (lambda **kw: 'DUMMY' at line 4021). No test monkeypatches run_sorter to raise. The finally block is python-guaranteed to run, but the sj_kwargs guard (line 1325 'if sj_kwargs') and the reset-then-reapply ordering (1334-1335) are nontrivial and would benefit from a raise-path test.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Standard try/finally semantics make this 'probably correct', but the audit-task asked specifically about job_kwargs threading; this is a real untested branch. A regression that moved the restore outside the finally would leak global state.

verdict.reasoning: Verified all four cited locations directly. v1 (sorting.py:452-455) does not touch global job kwargs; it splats sorter_params straight into sis.run_sorter. v2 (sorting.py:1304-1335) captures previous_global, installs sj_kwargs gated by `if sj_kwargs`, wraps run_sorter in try at 1322-1323, and restores via reset_global_job_kwargs() + set_global_job_kwargs(**previous_global) inside a finally gated on `if sj_kwargs`. The non-trivial reset-then-reapply ordering is documented at 1326-1333: since SI's set_global_job_kwargs UPDATES rather than REPLACES, keys absent from the default global (chunk_size/total_memory/chunk_memory) would otherwise leak. Test coverage check: grep across tests/spikesorting/v2/ found only one test exercising this path -- test_run_si_sorter_restores_global_job_kwargs at test_single_session_pipeline.py:4001 -- and it monkeypatches sis.run_sorter to `lambda **kw: "DUMMY"` (line 4021), i.e., success path only. No test patches run_sorter to raise. The raise path is structurally protected by python's try/finally semantics, so a regression is unlikely, but the audit-specific concern about job_kwargs threading and the non-trivial reset-then-reapply ordering make this a legitimate untested branch. Medium severity is appropriate -- not a current bug, but a refactor that moved the restore outside finally (or short-circuited it on raise) would leak global state across populates undetected.

confirmed_findings.title: _run_si_sorter: sorter_temp_dir.cleanup() failure can mask the upstream sort exception
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 creates sorter_temp_dir = tempfile.TemporaryDirectory(dir=temp_dir) without an explicit cleanup() call (sorting.py:419-420); GC handles teardown. If the sort fails, the original exception propagates; the tempdir cleanup happens later and any failure during it is swallowed by GC.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 wraps the sorter call in a try/finally and calls sorter_temp_dir.cleanup() in the outer finally (sorting.py:1336-1342). Python's exception machinery means that if cleanup() raises (permission error, subprocess holding a file), the original sort exception is REPLACED by the cleanup exception. The actual failure cause is lost.
confirmed_findings.evidence: No test injects a cleanup() failure. The docstring at sorting.py:1337-1341 calls out the explicit cleanup as a deliberate change ('survives worker-process exit predictably under the parallel-populate process pool') but does not address the exception-shadowing risk. v1's lazy GC-based cleanup would not have this problem.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: The explicit cleanup is correct for the parallel-populate pool. Recommend wrapping the cleanup in its own try/except that logs but does not re-raise -- so an actual sort failure surfaces with its original traceback. Or use ExitStack to preserve the primary exception.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly against both files. v1/sorting.py:419-420 creates the TemporaryDirectory with no explicit cleanup() call anywhere in the file (grep confirms only three references: creation, naming, and use as output_folder). v2/sorting.py:1247 opens a try: block that ends at 1336-1342 with a finally: sorter_temp_dir.cleanup(). Python try/finally semantics: if the finally raises while another exception is in flight, the new exception replaces the original in the propagated traceback (the original is retained as __context__ but the visible failure surface is the cleanup error). The docstring at 1337-1341 documents the deliberate choice ("survives worker-process exit predictably under the parallel-populate process pool") but does not discuss exception shadowing. No v2 test injects a cleanup failure: grep for PermissionError/OSError.*cleanup/mock.*cleanup against tests/spikesorting/v2/ returned no matches; existing "cleanup" hits are all about table-row cascade cleanup, not TemporaryDirectory.cleanup() failures. v1's GC-based teardown does swallow finalizer errors (CPython prints to stderr but does not raise into the propagating exception), so the regression is real. Severity low is appropriate: failure of cleanup() on a freshly-created, just-used scratch dir is uncommon, and the original exception is preserved as __context__ (just less visible). Recommended fix (try/except around cleanup, or contextlib.ExitStack) is sound.

confirmed_findings.title: noise_levels=None branch in _run_clusterless_thresholder pins random_seed but is not directly tested
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1's clusterless_thresholder default ALWAYS shipped noise_levels=[1.0] (sorting.py:177); there was no None branch.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2's _run_clusterless_thresholder at sorting.py:1146-1167 has a noise_levels-is-None branch that (a) pops noise_levels, (b) sets random_slices_kwargs={'seed': random_seed} so SI's get_noise_levels MAD estimate is deterministic across runs. This is the v2-introduced 'auto-MAD' path. Per the inline comment, the path was added to undo the v1 1400x clusterless bug -- it MUST be deterministic, but no direct unit test exercises this branch.
confirmed_findings.evidence: test_clusterless_detect_peaks_strips_random_seed (test_single_session_pipeline.py:4042) passes noise_levels=[1.0] -- it hits the ELSE branch (sorting.py:1168-1176), NOT the None branch. The canonical-form tests at test_parity_canonical.py:167-179 test fingerprint equivalence but do NOT drive runtime. No test verifies that with noise_levels=None and the same seed, two _run_clusterless_thresholder calls produce identical sortings on identical input. This is the EXACT determinism invariant the comment at sorting.py:1156-1167 claims to enforce.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Given the prior 1400x divergence bug in the noise_levels area, this branch is high-risk and should be tested. Direct unit test: call _run_clusterless_thresholder twice with noise_levels=None + same random_seed, assert identical sorted spike samples. The integration MS4 determinism test exists but does not isolate this branch.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly against source: v1 sorting.py:177 always ships noise_levels=np.asarray([1.0]); v2 sorting.py:1146-1167 introduces a noise_levels-is-None branch that pops the key and sets random_slices_kwargs={'seed': _random_seed} to pin SI's get_noise_levels MAD seed, with an extensive inline comment citing SI PR #3359 (seed=0 -> seed=None default change) and naming Spyglass as the explicit seeder. The only direct unit test that calls Sorting._run_clusterless_thresholder is test_single_session_pipeline.py:4042-4079 (test_clusterless_detect_peaks_strips_random_seed), which passes noise_levels=[1.0] and therefore hits the ELSE branch at sorting.py:1168-1176, not the None branch. The canonical-form tests at test_parity_canonical.py:167-179 only verify fingerprint-level equivalence of None vs absent; they do not drive runtime. The integration v1<->v2 parity test (test_single_session_pipeline.py:6680+) does exercise the None branch indirectly via SMOKE_CLUSTERLESS_PARAMS (_smoke_constants.py:31-43 intentionally omits noise_levels so Pydantic default => None), but it compares against pickled v1 baselines rather than asserting the specific determinism invariant the comment promises (two calls with noise_levels=None + same random_seed produce identical peaks). One nuance: the branch is not totally unreached at integration level, but the determinism-via-repeat-call assertion is genuinely missing. Given the prior 1400x clusterless divergence bug in this same area, the high severity rating is justified -- a direct twice-call-asserts-equal unit test on the None path is a clear gap.

refuted_findings.title: get_sorting zero-unit path uses NumpySorting.from_unit_dict({}, ...) instead of [{}] -- API-shape divergence vs tests
refuted_findings.why_refuted: Cited lines match: v1 sorting.py:539-541 uses [units_dict] (list); v2 sorting.py:763-765 uses bare {} positionally. But this is NOT a behavioral divergence. SpikeInterface 0.104.0 (the pinned version, verified against the GitHub source) explicitly normalizes the input inside NumpySorting.from_unit_dict: 'if isinstance(units_dict_list, dict): units_dict_list = [units_dict_list]'. So from_unit_dict({}, fs) and from_unit_dict([{}], fs) produce identical NumpySorting objects. This is a documented, supported polymorphism in SI's positional API, not a coercion accident. Furthermore, the tests DO exercise the production bare-dict path: test_single_session_pipeline.py:3518 calls Sorting().get_sorting(sort_pk) on the zero-unit row and asserts get_num_units() == 0 -- the assertion is sufficient because SI's normalization eliminates the alleged spurious-success failure mode. The tests themselves even use the bare-dict form at line 4989 (from_unit_dict({0: ..., 1: ...}, fs)) as canonical usage, demonstrating it's standard. The finding's concern that 'SI may interpret as units_dict_list (a dict, not a list of dicts) -- depending on SI 0.104's positional-arg coercion' is incorrect: the dict case is handled explicitly in 0.104.0 source. The v1/v2 asymmetry is purely cosmetic and has no behavioral impact. Refuted.

synthesis.executive_summary: v2 sorting.py is a substantial, deliberately scoped re-architecture of v1's monolithic sorter dispatch — the file grew from 602 to 1,648 lines because v2 absorbs concerns v1 either skipped (typed param validation, deterministic seeding, analyzer building) or got subtly wrong (singleton noise_levels, trailing-edge artifact mask off-by-one). The 53 confirmed audit findings are dominated by *intentional, justified* divergences. I found no unjustified semantic drift in default values or numerical behavior — the one historically dangerous default, clusterless `noise_levels=[1.0]`, is correctly preserved on the shipping default row at v2/sorting.py:175 while the schema-level default flipped to `None` to enable MAD semantics for synthetic fixtures. The most consequential changes are: (1) v1's auto-shipping ~18 SI-default rows is replaced by 7 explicit curated rows plus a generic fallback schema — a real migration cost for any v1 code that named a non-curated sorter via `('kilosort2_5','default')`; (2) the v1 trailing-edge artifact-mask off-by-one (last valid sample zeroed; last frame unmasked) is fixed via the v2 left-to-right complement walker at v2/sorting.py:1014-1027 with explicit `ms_before=None/ms_after=None` to avoid the SI 0.104 pad-zero boundary bug; (3) the SortingExtractor→SortingAnalyzer migration is genuinely new — v1's `sorting.py` contained no analyzer at all; v2 builds a `binary_folder` SortingAnalyzer at sort time (v2/sorting.py:1419-1448), with cleanup on three lifecycle events (make_compute failure, make_insert failure, delete cascade). The migration is sound but introduces a 5–50 GB scratch-folder lifecycle that v1 never managed, plus a `ZeroUnitAnalyzerError` carve-out (v2/sorting.py:841-848) that callers must handle. The top remaining audit concerns are the migration cost of the curated-row policy, the unstable trade-off in KS4's `extra="allow"` (SI default drift flows through silently), and a small number of low-severity Pydantic tightenings (int→float, gt=0 constraints) that are net positives but undocumented.

top_concerns.title: v1 auto-shipped ~18 SI-default rows; v2 ships only 7 explicit rows plus a generic fallback — silent breakage for v1 code naming non-curated sorters
top_concerns.why_it_matters: Any v1 user code that selects a sorter via `('kilosort2_5','default')`, `('ironclust','default')`, `('tridesclous','default')`, etc. relies on v1's dynamic insert loop at v1/sorting.py:184-189 that calls `sis.get_default_sorter_params(sorter)` for every SI-registered sorter. v2 ships only `mountainsort4` (2 presets), `mountainsort5`, `kilosort4`, `spykingcircus2`, `tridesclous2`, and `clusterless_thresholder` (7 rows total at v2/sorting.py:106-183). A user importing v1 workflows must explicitly insert the matching row; the generic `extra='allow'` schema (v2/_params/sorter.py:186-196) handles the validation but does not auto-populate. Failure mode is loud (FK violation at SortingSelection insert), not silent.
top_concerns.recommendation: Document the migration path in CHANGELOG / migration guide: for each non-curated v1 sorter row a workflow uses, the user must call `SorterParameters.insert1({'sorter': 'kilosort2_5', 'sorter_params_name': 'default', 'params': sis.get_default_sorter_params('kilosort2_5'), 'params_schema_version': 1})`. Consider adding an opt-in `insert_default_legacy_si_sorters()` classmethod that mirrors v1's behavior for users transitioning workflows in bulk.

top_concerns.title: Clusterless `noise_levels` schema default flipped from `[1.0]` to `None`; shipped default row still passes `[1.0]` — the 1,400× divergence bug is correctly fixed, but the schema-level flip changes implicit semantics
top_concerns.why_it_matters: This is the most safety-relevant finding. v1's `default_clusterless` row at v1/sorting.py:177 hard-codes `noise_levels=np.asarray([1.0])` so `detect_threshold=100` is interpreted in raw microvolts. v2's `ClusterlessThresholderSchema.noise_levels` default is `None` (v2/_params/sorter.py:159), which the runtime at v2/sorting.py:1146-1167 interprets as 'omit, let SI compute per-channel MAD' (with a deterministic seed pinned via PR #3359 rationale). A user constructing `ClusterlessThresholderSchema()` with no args now gets MAD semantics, not microvolt semantics — opposite of the v1 default behavior. The shipping default row at v2/sorting.py:166-175 explicitly opts back into `[1.0]` to preserve v1 microvolt semantics for production. The fix is correct and well-documented (schema docstring at _params/sorter.py:134-148 + regression test `test_clusterless_default_row_ships_noise_levels_one` at test_params_validation.py:285-313). Additionally, v2/sorting.py:1168-1176 broadcasts singleton `[1.0]` to `n_channels` via `np.full(...)` before the SI call — fixing a latent v1 bug where SI's `locally_exclusive` indexes `noise_levels[chan]` and would IndexError on multi-channel recordings.
top_concerns.recommendation: Surface the semantic flip in user-facing release notes with a 1-line warning: 'If you build clusterless params programmatically via ClusterlessThresholderSchema() without arguments, pass noise_levels=[1.0] explicitly to preserve v1 microvolt semantics; the default schema now uses MAD multiples.' The regression test at test_params_validation.py:285 already names the 1,400× divergence — keep that test as a long-term guard.
top_concerns.v2_location: src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_params/sorter.py:159 (schema default None); src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:166-175 (shipped row [1.0]); src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:1146-1176 (runtime broadcast + MAD-path seed pin)

top_concerns.title: v2 fixes v1's trailing-edge artifact-mask off-by-one (last valid sample zeroed, last frame n-1 left unmasked)
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v1's back-gap loop at v1/sorting.py:382-388 uses `np.arange(artifact_removed_intervals_ind[-1][1], len(timestamps) - 1)`. Because v1's `_consolidate_intervals` returns stop_index INCLUSIVE (uses `searchsorted(side='right') - 1` at v1/recording.py:743), starting the trigger at `stop_ind[-1]` rather than `stop_ind[-1] + 1` ZEROS the last valid sample of the trailing valid interval, AND ending at `len(timestamps) - 1` LEAVES the very last frame unmasked. v2 rewrites this entirely (v2/sorting.py:993-1055) as a left-to-right complement walker over the half-open valid_times intervals, using `searchsorted(side='left')` consistently and `end = len(timestamps)` for the back gap — both boundaries are now correct. v2 also explicitly uses `ms_before=None, ms_after=None` rather than `ms_before=0.0, ms_after=0.0` (documented at v2/sorting.py:1041-1048) because the latter triggers a separate SI 0.104 boundary bug where the first artifact frame in any contiguous run is left unmasked. Regression test `test_apply_artifact_mask_zeroes_artifact_frames` at tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:2904 pins both the zero-fill inside the gap AND the boundary frame.
top_concerns.recommendation: Note in the migration guide that v1 spike counts in the immediate vicinity of artifact-mask boundaries can differ from v2 outputs on the same recording — both the v1 last-valid-sample being zeroed and the v1 final-frame being unmasked produce small-but-real spike-count differences at the artifact edges. The test at test_single_session_pipeline.py:2904 is the appropriate long-term guard.

top_concerns.title: SortingExtractor → SortingAnalyzer migration: v1's `sorting.py` had no analyzer at all; v2 builds a binary_folder SortingAnalyzer at sort time with three new lifecycle cleanup paths
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v1's `sorting.py` contained no `SortingAnalyzer` / `WaveformExtractor` references (`grep -c` returns 0); v1 deferred waveform extraction to `metric_curation.py` (uses the deprecated `si.WaveformExtractor`) and `burst_curation.py`. v2 builds a `binary_folder` SortingAnalyzer at sort time inside `_build_analyzer` (v2/sorting.py:1419-1448) and computes `random_spikes`, `noise_levels`, `templates`, `waveforms` as base extensions. This introduces a 5–50 GB scratch-folder lifecycle that v1 never managed: cleanup paths at (a) make_compute failure (v2/sorting.py:614-626), (b) make_insert failure (v2/sorting.py:686-705), (c) `Sorting.delete` override (v2/sorting.py:924-958, with the `folders_to_remove` collected BEFORE the cascade-delete because the row needed to compute the path is gone after). A new `ZeroUnitAnalyzerError` (v2/sorting.py:841-848) is raised when a caller asks for the analyzer on a zero-unit sort (SI's `estimate_sparsity` crashes on `np.concatenate([])`). The migration is sound, but callers that previously assumed `get_sorting(key)` was the whole story now have a parallel `get_analyzer(key)` accessor with different failure modes and on-disk side effects.
top_concerns.recommendation: (1) Document that `Sorting.delete()` is mandatory for analyzer-folder cleanup — calling `(Sorting & key).delete()` directly works, but external scripts that bypass the override (`Sorting.connection.query(...)` style) will leak 5–50 GB per row. (2) Add a periodic disk-leak audit job that crosses `_analyzer_path` for every existing row against the filesystem and flags orphans; the existing `find_orphaned_masters` pattern at v2/sorting.py:358 is the right shape. (3) Document `ZeroUnitAnalyzerError` in the user-facing accessor docstring so callers know to handle it (v2/sorting.py:821-848 already documents it; consider promoting to module-level docstring).
top_concerns.v1_location: src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/sorting.py (no analyzer at all); waveform extraction at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/metric_curation.py:392 (deprecated `extract_waveforms`)

top_concerns.title: MS4 schema's `freq_min`/`freq_max` defaults silently choose v1's tetrode preset (600/6000) over SI's MS4 wrapper defaults (300/6000)
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v1's `mountain_default` block at v1/sorting.py:145-153 does NOT include `freq_min`/`freq_max` — those keys come from per-row presets: `freq_min=600` for tetrode (v1/sorting.py:159), `freq_min=300` for cortex (v1/sorting.py:164). v1's auto-shipped `('mountainsort4','default')` row uses SI wrapper defaults `freq_min=300`. v2's `MountainSort4Schema` bakes `freq_min=600.0` and `freq_max=6000.0` directly into the schema (v2/_params/sorter.py:58-59), so a user calling `MountainSort4Schema()` with no arguments gets the *tetrode* preset implicitly. The cortex preset row at v2/sorting.py:124-132 still overrides `freq_min=300` explicitly, so the cortex preset itself is correct. The choice is documented in the schema docstring (_params/sorter.py:38-51). The risk: a user who reads the schema field defaults expecting SI wrapper defaults gets tetrode-preset behavior, and the test at test_params_validation.py:208-218 does not pin freq_min/freq_max to specific values (only the non-freq fields are asserted).
top_concerns.recommendation: Add a regression test that pins `MountainSort4Schema().freq_min == 600.0` and `freq_max == 6000.0` to lock in the intentional choice — currently only the docstring records it. Mention in migration docs that user code constructing `MountainSort4Schema()` programmatically receives the tetrode preset.

top_concerns.title: MS5 v2 schema strips 12 of 20 SI-default fields under `extra='forbid'`; runtime falls back to SI defaults for stripped fields
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v1's auto-inserted `('mountainsort5','default')` row carries 20 fields from `sis.get_default_sorter_params('mountainsort5')`. v2's `MountainSort5Schema` exposes only 8 SI-facing fields + `schema_version` (v2/_params/sorter.py:68-88), and `extra='forbid'` rejects any attempt to set a stripped field at insert time. Four of the 12 strips are justified by the upstream-recording-already-filtered-and-whitened design choice (`filter`, `freq_min`, `freq_max`, `whiten` — documented at _params/sorter.py:71-77). The other 8 strips (`npca_per_channel`, `npca_per_subdivision`, `scheme1_detect_channel_radius`, `scheme2_max_num_snippets_per_training_batch`, `scheme2_training_duration_sec`, `scheme2_training_recording_sampling_mode`, `scheme3_block_duration_sec`, `snippet_mask_radius`) have no per-field rationale and fall through to SI's runtime defaults. A power-user wanting to tune e.g. `snippet_mask_radius` (default 250µm, relevant for tetrode geometry) or `scheme2_training_duration_sec` (default 300s, relevant for short recordings) hits `extra_forbidden`. The typo-rejection test at test_params_validation.py:328-348 confirms the `extra='forbid'` behavior but does not specifically test power-user override attempts.
top_concerns.recommendation: (1) Either document the 8 silently-stripped fields explicitly in the MS5 schema docstring, OR add them as opt-in fields with SI defaults. (2) If `extra='forbid'` is retained for the typo-catching value, mention in migration docs that MS5 tuning beyond the 8 typed fields requires schema editing — for the common case `snippet_mask_radius` this is a real Frank-lab knob worth surfacing.

top_concerns.title: KS4 schema's `extra='allow'` mirrors v1's escape hatch but lets SI upstream KS4 default drift through silently
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v2's `Kilosort4Schema` types only 5 KS4 fields with named defaults (`Th_universal=9.0`, `Th_learned=8.0`, `nblocks=1`, `max_cluster_subset=25_000`, `do_CAR=True`) and uses `extra='allow'` for the remaining ~25+ KS4 fields (v2/_params/sorter.py:91-112). The shipping default row at v2/sorting.py:140-146 only stores the 5 typed values + `schema_version`. All other KS4 fields fall through to SI's per-version defaults at sort time, so a SpikeInterface upgrade that changes (e.g.) `batch_size` or `nearest_chans` defaults silently changes v2 sort outputs unless the user supplied an explicit override. This matches v1's behavior because v1 auto-loaded SI defaults too — but v1's `('kilosort4','default')` row stored a snapshot of SI defaults at install time, whereas v2's stored params are skeletal so the drift is more direct. The escape hatch is documented (_params/sorter.py:91-104) and the contract is tested (`test_kilosort4_schema_accepts_extra_kwargs` at test_params_validation.py:351-364).
top_concerns.recommendation: Pin the SI version in `pyproject.toml` and add an integration test that snapshots `sis.get_default_sorter_params('kilosort4')` against a known-good version; flag SI upgrades when the upstream KS4 defaults shift. Alternatively, at the cost of more curation work, expand the typed KS4 field surface to cover the next ~10 most-relevant tuning knobs so default drift surfaces as version bumps rather than silent behavioral change.

top_concerns.title: Singleton `noise_levels=[1.0]` broadcast to `n_channels` at runtime — fixes a latent v1 bug that would IndexError on multi-channel recordings
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v1's `default_clusterless` at v1/sorting.py:177 stores `noise_levels: np.asarray([1.0])` (singleton) and at v1/sorting.py:411 passes it directly to `detect_peaks(recording, **sorter_params)`. SI's `locally_exclusive` peak-detection method indexes `noise_levels[chan] * detect_threshold` per channel, so a singleton array on a multi-channel recording either IndexErrors or silently misreads the wrong channel. v2's runtime at v2/sorting.py:1168-1176 explicitly broadcasts a singleton via `np.full(recording.get_num_channels(), float(nl[0]), dtype=np.float64)` BEFORE forwarding to `detect_peaks`. The fix is documented (v2/sorting.py:1112-1125) and tested at multiple sites (`test_clusterless_default_row_ships_noise_levels_one` at test_params_validation.py:285-309 names this as the regression guard for the 1,400× divergence). This is a real v1 bug being silently fixed by v2 — the v1 behavior on multi-channel recordings was undefined.
top_concerns.recommendation: Document the v1 latent bug in the v2 migration guide: v1 clusterless sorts on multi-channel recordings either crashed or produced incorrect noise-level scaling; v2 correctly scales per-channel. A v1→v2 numerical comparison on multi-channel clusterless sorts will show real, correct differences.

top_concerns.title: Deterministic seeding for SI's random-chunk paths (PR #3359 fallout) added in both `_run_si_sorter` and `_run_clusterless_thresholder` — not present in v1
top_concerns.why_it_matters: SI PR #3359 (merged 2024-10-25) changed SI's default `seed=0` to `seed=None` for `sip.whiten`'s covariance estimate and for `get_noise_levels`'s per-channel MAD estimation. v2 explicitly pins both: `_run_si_sorter` at v2/sorting.py:1281-1284 passes `seed=_random_seed` (default 0) to `sip.whiten`, and `_run_clusterless_thresholder` at v2/sorting.py:1164-1167 pins `random_slices_kwargs={'seed': _random_seed}` when `noise_levels=None`. The docstring (v2/sorting.py:1262-1280) records empirical evidence: 3 MS4 runs with seed=0 produced identical `(n_units, median_fr)`; 4 runs without the pin produced 4 distinct states. v1 did not pin either seed because v1 ran against the older SI defaults where `seed=0` was implicit. This is a correctness improvement, not a regression — but v1↔v2 spike-time comparisons on the same input will differ by ~10–20 borderline peaks per shank when SI is the post-#3359 version.
top_concerns.recommendation: Note in user docs that v2 is bit-reproducible across runs only when `random_seed` is held constant; the per-row `SorterParameters.job_kwargs` blob is the override mechanism. For variance-characterization studies, users override by passing `random_seed=N` in `job_kwargs`.
top_concerns.v1_location: src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/sorting.py:429 (`sip.whiten` with no seed); v1/sorting.py:411 (`detect_peaks` with no random_slices_kwargs)

top_concerns.title: v1's stale clusterless kwargs (`outputs`, `random_chunk_kwargs`) require defense-in-depth: schema rejects at insert, runtime strips defensively in case pre-existing rows slip through
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v1's `default_clusterless` row carries `outputs: 'sorting'` (a Spyglass-only routing hint never consumed by SI; stripped at v1/sorting.py:404) and `random_chunk_kwargs: {}` (renamed to `random_slices_kwargs` in SI 0.104, with the v1 keyword having no effect in the prior strip-and-call path). v2's `ClusterlessThresholderSchema` has `extra='forbid'` (v2/_params/sorter.py:152) and rejects both fields at insert time. The runtime at v2/sorting.py:1143-1144 additionally strips both keys defensively: `for stale in ('outputs', 'random_chunk_kwargs'): params.pop(stale, None)`. The schema_version was bumped 1→3 (v2: dropped these fields; v3: made `noise_levels` optional) so consumers detect the change explicitly. Tests `test_clusterless_schema_documents_dead_fields_or_drops_them` (test_v1_parity.py:281-288) and `test_clusterless_default_matches_v1` (test_params_validation.py:255-282) pin the contract.
top_concerns.recommendation: No action needed — the defense-in-depth is correct. Document in migration docs that v1 `default_clusterless` rows that still carry these fields literally inserted into v2 will fail validation; the runtime strip is a belt-and-braces for any rows that slip in via raw `Lookup.insert` bypass.

top_concerns.title: Sorting.Unit part table is entirely new in v2 — v1 sorting.py had no per-unit Spyglass row; brain-region resolution flows through `Sorting.Unit * Electrode * BrainRegion`
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v1's `SpikeSorting` table has no `.Unit` part — all per-unit metadata lives only inside the NWB Units table. v2 adds `Sorting.Unit` (v2/sorting.py:432-448) with FK to Electrode, `peak_amplitude_uv` (float, microvolts) and `n_spikes` (int). `_populate_unit_part` (v2/sorting.py:1548-1648) resolves the peak channel via `template_tools.get_template_extremum_channel(analyzer, outputs='id')` and maps to an Electrode row through `SortGroupV2.SortGroupElectrode`. The Unit-part FK enables `get_unit_brain_regions` (v2/sorting.py:960-989) with explicit `region_resolution` semantics ('single_session' vs 'anchor_member' for concat). Non-integer unit IDs raise `NonIntegerUnitIDError` (v2/sorting.py:1610-1618) — v1 silently accepted whatever sorter emitted. The contract is enforced inside the transaction (v2/sorting.py:679-685) so master + Unit-part commit atomically.
top_concerns.recommendation: Document in user-facing docs that v2 adds a per-unit row inside the DataJoint graph (not only in NWB), so downstream queries can join Sorting.Unit to Electrode and BrainRegion without re-reading the NWB. Note the integer-unit-id contract — non-numeric sorter IDs will fail at sort time rather than silently flowing through.

top_concerns.title: MS4 `detect_threshold` retyped int→float and constrained `gt=0`; benign coercion but adds insert-time validation v1 lacked
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v1 stores `detect_threshold: 3` (int) in `mountain_default` (v1/sorting.py:152) with no validation. v2 schema declares `detect_threshold: float = Field(default=3.0, gt=0.0)` (v2/_params/sorter.py:64). The float coercion is benign for SI's downstream numeric code; the `gt=0.0` constraint is defense-in-depth — no realistic v1 workflow sets `detect_threshold ≤ 0`, but a fat-finger insert in v1 would silently accept `0` or `-1`. The rationale is undocumented in the schema (only `extra='forbid'` is mentioned).
top_concerns.recommendation: Add a one-line schema docstring noting the int→float + gt=0 tightening so future contributors see the intentional choice rather than reading it as drift. No behavioral fix needed.

top_concerns.title: `peak_sign` widened to include `'both'` matching SI's actual surface; v1 docstring undercounted SI's API
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v1 docstring at v1/sorting.py:124 declares `peak_sign: enum('neg', 'pos')` but stores arbitrary strings without validation. SI's `detect_peaks` actually documents `'neg' | 'pos' | 'both'` and asserts on those three values (SpikeInterface peak_detection.py:392, 482). v2's `ClusterlessThresholderSchema.peak_sign: Literal['neg', 'pos', 'both']` (v2/_params/sorter.py:156) matches SI's actual contract. This is a permissive widening that v1 would have silently accepted anyway via the lack of validation — no migration risk.
top_concerns.recommendation: No action needed; widening is correct. Consider one-line schema-docstring note acknowledging the `'both'` addition matches SI's API for traceability.

top_concerns.title: `get_sorting` zero-unit path uses `NumpySorting.from_unit_dict({}, ...)` (bare dict) vs v1's `[{}]` (list-wrapped); SI 0.104 API-shape adapt, not divergence
top_concerns.why_it_matters: v1 at sorting.py:539-541 calls `si.NumpySorting.from_unit_dict([units_dict], sampling_frequency=sampling_frequency)` (list-wrapped). v2 at sorting.py:763-765 calls `si.NumpySorting.from_unit_dict({}, sampling_frequency=fs)` (bare dict). Both are valid in SpikeInterface 0.104 — the `from_unit_dict` API accepts either a single segment as a dict or a multi-segment list of dicts. v2's zero-unit path returns an empty NumpySorting explicitly because `NwbSortingExtractor` cannot open an empty units NWB. The other audit attempt to flag this as divergence was correctly REFUTED in the input.
top_concerns.recommendation: No action — the audit input correctly refuted the divergence. Consider adding a brief comment at v2/sorting.py:763 noting that the dict-vs-list shape is a SI API convenience, not a behavior change.

intentional_changes_well_justified.title: Pydantic dispatch + insert1 validation replaces v1's `sorter_params: blob`
intentional_changes_well_justified.rationale: v1's `SpikeSorterParameters` table at v1/sorting.py:138-144 stores `sorter_params: blob` with no insert-time validation; any typo (`detect_signe`, `freq_mn`) silently lands and only fails at sort time hours later. v2's `SorterParameters.insert1` at v2/sorting.py:97-104 dispatches to `_get_sorter_schema(row['sorter'])` and validates the params dict before the row hits the DB. Per-sorter Pydantic schemas (v2/_params/sorter.py:35-196) catch typos at insert time for the curated set (MS4, MS5, KS4, SC2, TDC2, clusterless_thresholder); `GenericSorterParamsSchema` with `extra='allow'` is the fallback for any installed SI sorter — preserving v1's escape hatch. The `_assert_schema_version_matches` check (v2/sorting.py:101-103) ensures stored rows do not silently invalidate when a schema bumps version.

intentional_changes_well_justified.title: Tri-part `make_fetch` / `make_compute` / `make_insert` dispatch with parallel populate
intentional_changes_well_justified.rationale: v1's `make` is monolithic. v2 splits Sorting.make into three phases (v2/sorting.py:457-705) so the long-running sort runs OUTSIDE the framework transaction (sorters routinely take 5–20 minutes; an in-transaction sort holds row locks the entire time). `_parallel_make = True` (v2/sorting.py:455) enables non-daemon process-pool parallel populate. The fetch/compute/insert split also enforces a no-DB-I/O-inside-compute invariant — `obs_intervals` is pre-fetched in `make_fetch` (v2/sorting.py:499-514) and passed through to `make_compute`, mirroring v1 at v1/sorting.py:597 but without breaking the tri-part contract.

intentional_changes_well_justified.title: SortingSelection.RecordingSource / ConcatenatedRecordingSource part-table polymorphism
intentional_changes_well_justified.rationale: v1's `SpikeSortingSelection` has a single `recording_id` FK. v2 introduces source-part polymorphism (v2/sorting.py:222-234) so the schema can stably express both single-session and concat sorts; today `insert_selection` rejects the concat path with `NotImplementedError` (v2/sorting.py:271-276), but the schema is final so the validator relaxes without a migration once the concat materializer lands. `resolve_source` (v2/sorting.py:368-406) is the Layer-2 dispatch that lets `Sorting.make` route on source shape without inspecting raw part tables.

intentional_changes_well_justified.title: Three-event analyzer-folder lifecycle cleanup (make_compute fail, make_insert fail, delete cascade)
intentional_changes_well_justified.rationale: v1 has no analyzer folder. v2's `Sorting._build_analyzer` creates a 5–50 GB binary_folder per sort; without explicit cleanup the disk fills. v2 closes all three lifecycle events: make_compute except block (v2/sorting.py:614-626), make_insert except block (v2/sorting.py:686-705), and `Sorting.delete` override (v2/sorting.py:924-958) that collects folder paths BEFORE the cascade delete so cleanup can run after the row is gone. `ignore_errors=False` surfaces permission errors loudly rather than swallowing them silently.

intentional_changes_well_justified.title: Job-kwargs resolution centralized in `_resolved_job_kwargs`; `random_seed` carved out
intentional_changes_well_justified.rationale: v1 has no centralized job-kwargs path. v2's `_resolved_job_kwargs` (called once at v2/sorting.py:581) produces a dict that flows into BOTH `sis.run_sorter` AND `analyzer.compute` so n_jobs/chunk_duration/progress_bar overrides propagate to both stages from one resolution. The `random_seed` Spyglass-side knob is correctly stripped before installing as a SI global job kwarg (v2/sorting.py:1297-1303) because SI's `set_global_job_kwargs` rejects unknown keys; the same strip applies in the analyzer.compute path (v2/sorting.py:1434-1436). Without this carve-out, the populate would AssertionError on `random_seed is not a valid job keyword argument`.

intentional_changes_well_justified.title: Schema-version bookkeeping field stripped before SI dispatch
intentional_changes_well_justified.rationale: v2 stores `schema_version` on every params blob (v2/_params/sorter.py:55, 80, 107, 153) so the `_assert_schema_version_matches` check at insert time and at make_fetch time pins which schema validated the row. SI sorter wrappers do not accept the field; v2/sorting.py:565-568 explicitly strips it before dispatching. This is the kind of small-but-load-bearing detail that would silently fail with `Invalid parameters: ['schema_version']` if forgotten.

intentional_changes_well_justified.title: External float64 whitening matches v1's behavior; sorter's internal whiten flag turned off
intentional_changes_well_justified.rationale: v2/sorting.py:1260-1285 applies `sip.whiten(recording, dtype=np.float64, seed=_random_seed)` externally BEFORE dispatching the sorter, then sets `sorter_params['whiten'] = False` so the sorter doesn't double-whiten. This matches v1 at v1/sorting.py:428-430 exactly, with the added deterministic seed pin (PR #3359 rationale). Runs AFTER the artifact mask so masked-zero frames don't bias the whitening covariance estimate — a subtlety v1 also got right but didn't document.

intentional_changes_well_justified.title: v2 fixes `obs_intervals` to flow into every unit NWB row
intentional_changes_well_justified.rationale: v1 at v1/sorting.py:587-598 writes `obs_intervals=obs_interval` on every `add_unit` call. v2 mirrors this exactly at v2/sorting.py:1524-1529 with a defensive `obs_intervals=None` → full-recording fallback (v2/sorting.py:1480-1488). Downstream firing-rate computations need this column to know which segments of the recording the sort actually observed; without it the units NWB looks like the unit was observed across the full session even where the artifact mask blanked the signal.

intentional_changes_well_justified.title: `curation_label='uncurated'` scalar default mirrors v1 pre-curation shape
intentional_changes_well_justified.rationale: v1's pre-curation NWB has `curation_label` as a SCALAR `'uncurated'` (v1/sorting.py:583-598), refined to a per-unit ragged-list shape by curation. v2 explicitly preserves the v1 pre-vs-post-curation shape discontinuity (v2/sorting.py:1494-1517) so external readers expecting v1's shape — `nwb.units['curation_label'][i] == 'uncurated'` — continue to work. The discontinuity is inherited from v1 and intentional.

intentional_changes_well_justified.title: Zero-unit sorting handled as a valid result, not an exception
intentional_changes_well_justified.rationale: A sorter that finds no units is a legitimate scientific outcome (low-amplitude recording, aggressive threshold). v1 has a `nwbf.units = pynwb.misc.Units(...)` guard at v1/sorting.py:578-581. v2 extends this in three places: (1) `_build_analyzer` short-circuits before any I/O (v2/sorting.py:1396-1403) because SI's `estimate_sparsity` crashes on `np.concatenate([])`; (2) `_populate_unit_part` returns early (v2/sorting.py:1571-1572); (3) `get_sorting` returns an empty NumpySorting (v2/sorting.py:749-765) rather than crashing on `NwbSortingExtractor` which can't open an empty units NWB; (4) `get_analyzer` raises a clear `ZeroUnitAnalyzerError` (v2/sorting.py:841-848). All four paths are necessary because SI's behavior on zero-unit inputs is inconsistent.

recommended_actions: Add a one-line `MountainSort4Schema` schema-docstring note pinning the int→float + `gt=0` tightening on `detect_threshold` so future contributors see the intentional choice rather than reading it as drift (no behavioral fix needed).

Add a regression test that pins `MountainSort4Schema().freq_min == 600.0` and `freq_max == 6000.0` — currently only the docstring records the intentional tetrode-preset choice; test_params_validation.py:208-218 does not assert these specific values.

Document the curated-row migration cost in CHANGELOG / migration docs: any v1 workflow naming a non-curated sorter (`kilosort2_5`, `ironclust`, `tridesclous`, `klusta`, `herdingspikes`, etc.) via `('sorter_name','default')` must explicitly `SorterParameters.insert1({...})` the matching row in v2. Consider an opt-in `insert_default_legacy_si_sorters()` classmethod that calls `sis.get_default_sorter_params` for each `available_sorters()` entry, replicating v1's behavior for bulk migration.

Document the clusterless `noise_levels` semantic flip in user-facing release notes with a 1-line warning: callers constructing `ClusterlessThresholderSchema()` programmatically without arguments now get MAD-multiple semantics instead of v1's raw-microvolt semantics; pass `noise_levels=[1.0]` explicitly to preserve v1 behavior.

Note in migration docs that v1↔v2 spike counts can differ near artifact-mask boundaries because v1 had a trailing-edge off-by-one (last valid sample zeroed; final frame unmasked) that v2 fixed. Also note that v1↔v2 clusterless sorts on multi-channel recordings will differ because v1's singleton `noise_levels=[1.0]` either crashed or silently misread channels; v2 correctly broadcasts to `n_channels`.

Consider adding a periodic disk-leak audit job that crosses `_analyzer_path` for every existing Sorting row against the filesystem and flags orphans (using the existing `find_orphaned_masters` pattern at v2/sorting.py:358 as the shape).

Pin the SpikeInterface version in `pyproject.toml` and add an integration test that snapshots `sis.get_default_sorter_params('kilosort4')` against a known-good version; flag SI upgrades when upstream KS4 defaults shift (because `extra='allow'` lets the defaults flow through silently). Same recommendation applies to MS5's 8 silently-stripped fields.

Surface the 8 silently-stripped MS5 fields (`npca_per_channel`, `npca_per_subdivision`, `scheme1_detect_channel_radius`, `scheme2_max_num_snippets_per_training_batch`, `scheme2_training_duration_sec`, `scheme2_training_recording_sampling_mode`, `scheme3_block_duration_sec`, `snippet_mask_radius`) either by documenting them explicitly in the MS5 schema docstring or by adding them as opt-in fields with SI defaults — `snippet_mask_radius` in particular is a real Frank-lab tuning knob for tetrode geometry that currently requires schema editing to override.

Add a brief comment at v2/sorting.py:763 noting that `NumpySorting.from_unit_dict({}, ...)` (bare dict) vs v1's `[{}]` (list-wrapped) is a SI API convenience — both shapes are valid in SI 0.104 — to prevent future readers from re-flagging this as a divergence (per the correctly-refuted audit finding).
